name: HP Inc.

profile.description: HP Inc. is the world's leading provider of personal computers and printing solutions, operating in Personal Systems (PC hardware, workstations) and Printing (consumer and commercial printers, ink/toner supplies). Q2 FY2026 revenue was $14.4B (+9% YoY) with EPS of $0.86 (+20% YoY), marking the eighth consecutive quarter of revenue growth. AI PC mix has risen to 44% of shipments, targeting 60-70% next fiscal year. Interim CEO Bruce Broussard leads following Enrique Lores' departure.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): HP's AI PC strategy has achieved meaningful commercial traction — AI PC mix grew from 35% to 44% of shipments in two quarters, driving Personal Systems revenue up 13% YoY in Q2 FY2026 [1][2]. The OpenAI partnership for EliteBook AI agent integration and the $1B AI-driven efficiency savings target by FY2028 represent genuine innovation. However, printing revenue was flat YoY and the core business model (PCs + printing supplies) is unchanged from a decade ago. AI Deployment (1/2): HP is deploying AI at operational scale — the $1B efficiency savings target from 4,000-6,000 headcount reductions through AI automation is a specific, quantified internal AI deployment [2]. No AI product revenue separately reported. Market Share (1/2): HP remains a leading PC OEM alongside Dell and Lenovo but has not gained definitive share; the AI PC push differentiates modestly [1]. Product Pipeline (1/2): AI PC roadmap targeting 60-70% of shipments by FY2027 provides specific pipeline visibility, but printing segment lacks next-gen product innovation [2]. R&D Intensity (1/1): R&D spend is competitive for IT hardware.

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): HP's purpose — 'to create technology that makes life better for everyone, everywhere' — is clear and connects products to human empowerment but is not distinctive enough to differentiate HP from any peer technology company. Community Impact (1/2): HP's circular economy leadership (5B+ pounds recycled materials since 2019, supporting 1,360+ waste-collection workers in Brazil) and MSCI AA ESG rating demonstrate genuine community investment with specific programs and dollar amounts [4]. Employee Retention (1/2): Glassdoor reviews describe a collaborative culture and meaningful projects, but also note 'nearly complete turnover on senior leadership in the past eighteen months' and 'countless reorganizations with layoffs,' suggesting average retention at best [3]. Insider Buying (1/2): No significant insider buying or selling data available; neutral.

infrastructure.label: STRONG
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics. Q2 FY2026 delivered $14.4B revenue (+9% YoY, eighth consecutive quarter of growth) with non-GAAP EPS of $0.86 (+20% YoY) and gross margin of 20.9% [2]. Forward P/E of 9.1x is deeply below the 20x target, reflecting market skepticism. Full-year guidance of $2.90-$3.10 non-GAAP EPS with $2.8-3.0B free cash flow [2]. Dividend yield of 4.44% with 11 consecutive years of growth and consistent payment history. Metric scores: Rev YoY 9% (0.97), Rev 5yr CAGR -4.2% (0.05), Fwd P/E 9.1 (1.00), Profit Margin 4.5% (0.22), Payout Ratio 43.7% (0.94, 1x), Consec Yrs Paid 11+ (1.00, 2x), Consec Yrs Growth 11 (1.00, 1x), Div Growth CAGR 9.1% (1.00, 2x). D/E and ROE excluded (null). Weighted sum: 8.18/10 = 8.2, rounded to 8.

ai_resilience.label: MODERATE
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (5/9): HP faces genuine AI disruption risk in its core printing business — AI-generated content, digital workflows, and paperless adoption are structurally reducing print volumes, threatening the highest-margin supplies revenue stream [2]. In PCs, AI is a double-edged sword: the AI PC refresh cycle drove 13% Personal Systems growth in Q2 FY2026, but AI tools could extend PC replacement cycles by making older hardware more productive. Glassdoor reviews note an 'irrational push toward AI at the expense of talented engineers' suggesting the AI transition is creating internal tension [3]. The printing supplies business remains most vulnerable to secular decline. AI Opportunity (0/1): While HP embeds AI features in PCs via the OpenAI partnership, it does not separately quantify AI-related revenue and is a consumer of AI technology rather than a provider.

infinite_game.summary: HP has navigated multiple existential cycles — the 2015 split from HPE, PC market collapses, and now the AI transition — demonstrating strategic resilience. However, the abrupt CEO departure (Lores leaving without a permanent replacement), $650M restructuring with 4,000-6,000 job cuts framed as 'AI efficiency,' and Glassdoor reports of 'countless reorganizations with layoffs' suggest a company responding to financial pressure rather than proactively building for the future. The interim CEO situation creates strategic uncertainty.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): HP's cause — 'creating technology that makes life better for everyone, everywhere' — traces back to the original HP garage founding in 1939 and has been applied through calculators, PCs, printers, and now AI PCs [4]. The circular materials commitment (5B+ pounds since 2019) and sustainability leadership (MSCI AA ESG rating) extend the purpose beyond hardware transactions. However, the cause has not been clearly validated through the current leadership transition — it reads more as inherited legacy than active strategic compass.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (0/2): Glassdoor reviews describe 'nearly complete turnover on senior leadership in the past eighteen months' and 'countless reorganizations with layoffs,' indicating a low-trust environment [3]. The CEO departure, 4,000-6,000 headcount reduction, and $650M restructuring charge create organizational anxiety inconsistent with Sinek's Circle of Safety. Employees face uncertainty about direction under interim leadership with no permanent CEO named.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): HP explicitly benchmarks against Dell, Lenovo, and Apple in competitive positioning, and the AI PC strategy was accelerated by Microsoft's Copilot+ PC initiative and Apple's M-series processor performance — evidence of competitors driving HP's improvement rather than a desire to 'win' against them [2].

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): The 2015 split from Hewlett-Packard Enterprise was a genuine existential pivot that separated consumer and printing businesses from enterprise hardware and services. HP Inc.'s subsequent pivot to AI PCs and subscription printing services represents ongoing model evolution, though incremental rather than transformative [1].

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (0/2): The $650M restructuring and 4,000-6,000 job cuts framed as AI efficiency are financially driven cost-cutting, not courageous long-term bets [2]. Lores' abrupt departure without a permanent successor suggests leadership instability rather than principled decision-making. The interim CEO (former Humana CEO Bruce Broussard) is a caretaker appointment, not a bold leadership choice for an AI hardware transition.

investment_thesis: HP offers an attractive value proposition with a 4.44% dividend yield, $2.8-3.0B annual free cash flow, and 9.1x forward P/E — the eighth consecutive quarter of revenue growth (+9% YoY) and 20% EPS growth in Q2 FY2026 demonstrate the AI PC refresh cycle is real and the market is not fully pricing it [1][2]. The $1B in targeted AI-driven cost savings by FY2028, combined with 11 consecutive years of dividend growth and $0.30/share quarterly payout, make HP a compelling capital return story at current valuation. AI PC mix rising from 35% to 44% in two quarters with a 60-70% target by FY2027 provides multi-year revenue visibility.
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on secular printing decline, memory inflation, and leadership vacuum. Printing revenue was flat YoY while DRAM/NAND prices have compressed Personal Systems margins — management expects PS margins below long-term range for the remainder of FY2026 due to memory cost pressures [2]. The CEO transition creates strategic uncertainty — HP is executing a major restructuring, OpenAI partnership, and AI PC transition simultaneously without permanent leadership. Profit margin of 4.5% is thin for a $55B revenue company, and the 5-year revenue CAGR of -4.2% reveals that the recent growth is a recovery from contraction rather than structural expansion. PC unit TAM could decline if tariffs or macro weakness hit consumer demand, reversing momentum.
key_catalysts: AI PC mix reaching 60-70% of shipments by FY2027 could drive sustained mid-single-digit revenue growth and premium ASPs as enterprises complete Windows 11 refresh cycles
Memory cost normalization — DRAM/NAND prices returning to historical ranges would restore $300-400M in annual gross profit and expand Personal Systems margins back to long-term targets
Permanent CEO appointment providing strategic clarity and potentially unlocking a re-rating if the market gains confidence in HP's AI and printing transformation roadmap
key_risks: Memory inflation persistence — DRAM/NAND at 35% of PC BOM through FY2027 would keep PS margins below long-term range and force guidance reductions, compressing the already-low 9.1x forward multiple

Printing secular decline accelerating as AI-generated content and digital workflows reduce print volumes — the highest-margin segment has no visible growth catalyst and flat revenue masks deteriorating unit economics

CEO transition risk — interim CEO Broussard (former Humana CEO) has no hardware industry experience, and a prolonged search or strategic pivot by the permanent replacement could disrupt AI PC execution and trigger multiple compression

HP Inc. Q2 FY2026 Earnings Call Transcript — Investing.com, May 2026
